title: Former Miss Hall’s School teacher at center of grooming allegations is charged with rape
author: Abby Patkin
bullets: Matthew Rutledge, former teacher at Miss Hall's School, indicted on three counts of rape in connection with grooming allegations.
Rutledge accused of sexually assaulting students; arraignment set for April 22 according to Berkshire County District Attorney's Office.
Investigation linked Rutledge to multiple acts of misconduct from 1990s to 2010; school criticized for not protecting students.

title: FIFA reduces hotel room bookings in World Cup cities, including Boston
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: FIFA is reducing hotel room bookings in World Cup host cities, impacting locations like Boston.
Meet Boston anticipated the move, citing inflated initial estimates as a key factor.
Released hotel rooms can now be sold to transient visitors, potentially benefiting local hotels financially.

title: ‘I had just lost everything’: Kelsey Fitzsimmons testifies in her trial
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Kelsey Fitzsimmons, former North Andover officer, testifies in Essex County Superior Court accused of assault with a dangerous weapon.
Fitzsimmons was shot by colleague Patrick Noonan in June 2025 while officers served a restraining order from her ex-fiancé.
Judge Jeffrey Karp oversees the bench trial; both sides rested, and closing arguments are set for Thursday morning.

title: In the midst of the MVP race, Jaylen Brown explains his stance on the NBA’s 65-game rule
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Jaylen Brown discussed the NBA's 65-game rule for postseason awards, suggesting a lower threshold might be appropriate.
Brown acknowledged the necessity of a games-played requirement to prevent players with fewer games from gaining award advantages.
Brown is set to be the first top-five MVP contender to meet the 65-game requirement this season.

title: ‘It felt like a gut punch’: Pride flags removed from BU offices ignite free speech, inclusion debate
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Boston University removed Pride flags from campus offices citing a 'content-neutral' signage policy, sparking free speech debate.
Faculty argue the policy isn't 'content-neutral,' pointing to other unremoved signs like Taylor Swift posters in public windows.
BU claims the policy prevents signage from representing institutional views, while critics call for consistent enforcement and inclusion.

title: David Ortiz won Boston.com’s bracket of the best sports soundbites, and it wasn’t close
author: Hayden Bird
bullets: David Ortiz's 'This is our f****** city' won Boston.com's best sports soundbite bracket in 2026.
Ortiz's remark at the 2013 Fenway Park memorial for Marathon bombing victims dominated the competition.
Kevin Millar's 'Don’t let us win today’ unexpectedly reached the final round, upsetting higher seeds.

title: A JetBlue flight reportedly hit a coyote during takeoff at T.F. Green Airport
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: A JetBlue flight hit a coyote during takeoff at T.F. Green Airport on March 24.
The Airbus A320 returned safely to Warwick for inspection before continuing to JFK after being cleared.
The runway remained open, and the incident did not impact other airport flights.

title: Boston runner to take on 2026 Marathon for Corey C. Griffin Foundation
author: Kelly Chan
bullets: Annie Yardley, a Boston resident, will run the 2026 Boston Marathon for the Corey C. Griffin Foundation.
Yardley, 30, admits she wasn't an athlete before but found motivation during her marathon training.
The Corey C. Griffin Foundation supports education and healthcare access for children, which inspires Yardley.

title: Cape Cod man charged with sister’s murder
author: John Waller
bullets: Kenneth Beltrame, 67, is charged with murder after his sister, Jennifer Beltrame, 61, was found dead in Sandwich.
Kenneth Beltrame is expected to be arraigned in Barnstable District Court following the discovery of his sister's body.
The Cape & Islands District Attorney reports Jennifer Beltrame showed signs of violent trauma; investigation is ongoing.

title: No one can use a gas-powered leaf blower in these 2 Mass. towns, thanks to new laws
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: Arlington and Lexington ban gas-powered leaf blowers, effective March 15, to address noise and environmental concerns.
Exceptions in Arlington and Lexington allow gas-powered use during emergencies and on properties over one acre with specific equipment.
Violations in Arlington and Lexington incur fines up to $300, with enforcement through complaint-based systems and police officers.
